title: Chapter Two: The Third Son
content:
## JEONG YEON-SHIN

Fifteen years later.

The wood was oak, old and dense, cut from a beam that had once supported the grain storehouse before it collapsed in the spring floods three years past. Yeon-shin had sawn it into a rectangle, bored holes in either end, and threaded a rope through for a handle. It weighed as much as a grown man.

He lifted it.

Feet shoulder-width, knees soft, spine straight. He pressed the beam overhead, held it for a count of ten, then lowered it to his shoulders and dropped into a horse stance so deep his thighs were parallel to the earth. He breathed in through his nose -- long, measured -- and on the exhale felt the internal energy pulse outward along his meridians, reinforcing muscle and tendon.

*Thirty-seven. Thirty-eight. Thirty-nine.*

He had been at this since before dawn. The training ground behind the east wing was his — no one else used it. The practice dummies were rotting, the sword rack held three dull blades, and the ground was more mud than earth. None of that mattered. No one came here to bother him.

*Forty.*

He set the beam down. Straightened. Pulled on the rough cotton shirt he had discarded at the start of the session. He was fifteen, lean, hard-muscled. Not large -- shorter than his eldest brother by a head -- but compact. Dense. Built like a man twice his years who had spent those years at the forge.

*The Jeong Family Dynamic Technique. Finished.*

His own creation. The family's meditation method was crude -- sit cross-legged, breathe, hope the energy moved. He had tried it for two years. The energy came easily, more easily than it should have, but the technique was a blunt instrument.

So he had taken it apart. Rebuilt the entire system around movement. Squatting, pressing, striking, running -- forcing the energy to flow through exertion, reinforcing muscle and tendon and bone in real time.

He had tried to share it, once.

*That was a mistake.*

He had been twelve. Still naive enough to believe that sharing blood meant something. He had gone to his brothers -- Nam-san the eldest, already growing into their father's belligerence, and Jung-san the second, careful, always watching which way the wind blew.

"I created a new martial art," Yeon-shin had said. "A dynamic technique. It circulates energy while you move. I think it could —"

"You created martial arts." Nam-san had stared at him as if he had announced he could fly.

"What if someone practices it and goes mad?" Jung-san had added, his voice carrying that particular careful venom that was worse than open hostility because it wore the mask of concern. "Will you take responsibility if someone dies from qi deviation?"

"Do you think," Nam-san had said, leaning forward, "you have the qualities of a *great master*?"

The words had landed like stones.

Great master. The founder of a style worthy of establishing a sect. Zhang Sanfeng. Bodhidharma. Half-legend, half-divine.

He had only wanted his brothers to be stronger.

Their mockery had been sincere, and sincerity was the sharpest blade.

He stopped sharing after that. Adopted the face he wore for the family -- blank, dull, faintly stupid. He called it the still-face. His mother's legacy, perhaps. The servants whispered that her people could watch their children die and not blink.

*Cold outside. Still inside. Let them see what they want to see.*

"Young master! It's time for the banquet!"

Yeon-shin did not look up. He finished his last set -- deep squats, the beam across his shoulders, each one slow and controlled -- set the weight down, wiped his hands on his trousers, and walked toward the compound.

The banquet was for Jung-san. He was to leave for Mount Zhongnan, for the Southern Edge Sect -- one of the Nine Great Sects. Their grandfather had been a secular disciple there, and the family had maintained the connection through decades of donations. Now the investment was bearing fruit: Jung-san had been accepted.

The banquet hall was full. The family head sat at the head of the long table, iron-grey topknot, rigid satisfaction on his face -- the closest Dae-myeong ever came to joy. The wives flanked him. Nam-san to the right with his wife and daughter, Jung-san the guest of honour, then the sisters, cousins, retainers, the steward.

Yeon-shin sat at the far end.

*As long as the food is good.*

This was the arrangement. Third son of a dead concubine, the boy who had killed his mother in the birthing. Fed, housed, tolerated. Placed at the bottom of the table, below even his younger half-siblings.

It suited him. No duties. No obligations. Just the training ground and the long quiet hours of muscle and breath.

"You're late, fool," Nam-san said as he entered.

"It's been a while, brother." Yeon-shin kept his voice mild. He sat. He picked up his chopsticks.

The family head raised his cup.

"Since my late father, who was a disciple of the Southern Edge Sect, founded our family," Dae-myeong began, his voice carrying the ponderous weight of a man who had rehearsed his speech, "I have devoted my life to reviving our clan. If I had wanted to make a name for myself in the world, I too would have inherited the Taoist techniques. But how could I abandon the family business?"

*You could not have inherited anything. Grandfather was a secular disciple, not a Taoist master. You know this. Everyone at this table knows this.*

Yeon-shin kept the still-face on. He ate.

"I am overjoyed," the family head continued, "that my son will follow in my father's footsteps and climb Mount Zhongnan."

Cups were raised. Compliments were offered. Jung-san accepted them with the careful modesty of a man who had been coached.

"Wouldn't the Southern Edge Sect be considered half a rank above Mount Hua these days?" someone asked.

"The Cloud Sword Dragon beheaded the leader of the evil sect forces at the Shaanxi tournament," Jung-san said, and Yeon-shin noted that he was already calling the sect's rising star *senior brother*, as if the acceptance letter were a coronation. "The Plum Blossom Sword Masters are strong, but they are no match for Senior Brother Sword Dragon."

*You have never held a sword against a man who wanted to kill you. But by all means, assess the relative merits of masters who could cut you in half.*

Yeon-shin reached for a rice cake.

Stopped.

The family head was looking at him. The look was the one Dae-myeong reserved for servants who had spilled wine or dogs that had tracked mud across the threshold — flat, cold, and faintly disgusted.

"I hear the third spends all day in the training ground," Dae-myeong said. "How is your progress with the Jeong Family Divine Sword?"

Yeon-shin set down his chopsticks. Straightened his back. Placed his hands on his knees.

"The principles of our family's martial arts are difficult for me to grasp, Father. I struggle to make progress. I am ashamed."

A lie. The Jeong Family Divine Sword was a patchwork -- crudely adapted from the Southern Edge Sect's techniques by a man who had learned them imperfectly. The sword paths were wrong. The footwork contradicted the cuts. He could have fixed it. But the memory of his brothers' faces had cured him of that impulse.

"The first and second sons are already approaching mastery," Dae-myeong said. "Your efforts are commendable, I suppose. But you should reflect on whether you are truly putting in effort, or merely going through the motions."

"I will heed your counsel, Father."

He bowed. He waited until the family head's gaze moved on. He picked up his chopsticks again.

*Showing interest will only hurt me.*

He ate steadily, face blank, and listened.

"The evil sect incident in Guanzhong has me concerned," the family head was saying to the steward Bak. "Are the intelligence networks in order?"

"We can cover Xinye," Bak said. "Perhaps as far as Xiangyang, if we stretch."

"Good. Good."

And then — a shout from the gate.

"Southern Edge masters have arrived!"

The banquet hall erupted. Chairs scraped. Cups were set down. The family head, who had been sitting with the immovable solidity of a temple guardian, shot to his feet and was out the door before the echo of the gatekeeper's voice had faded, his qinggong carrying him across the courtyard in three long strides.

Everyone followed.

Yeon-shin went with them.

*I'm curious.*

Three figures stood at the gate. Two men in Taoist robes of deep blue, their swords worn across their backs in the Zhongnan fashion, the hilts wrapped in white silk. And at the front, a woman.

She was an Elf.

Golden hair past her shoulders. Pointed ears, leaf-shaped, catching the lamplight. A face that was precise and ageless. She wore the blue robes of her companions, a sleeveless grey coat over them, silver thread at the hem.

She was smiling. It did not reach her eyes.

"I am Yeo Il-shin," she said. "Elder of the Southern Edge Sect. Forgive the sudden arrival."

The family head bowed so deeply his forehead nearly touched his knees.

"I — I am at a loss — such esteemed guests — I am Jeong Dae-myeong, head of the Jeong family —"

Behind him, the family dropped to their knees. Some pressed their foreheads to the ground. An Elf elder of a Nine Sect was living history.

Yeon-shin bowed with his hands clasped. Moderately. Respectfully.

*Not flat on my face, though.*

He raised his head.

Her eyes were on him.

Pale amber, flecked with gold. The gaze lasted a heartbeat. Then it moved on.

Something about him had caught her attention. He did not know what.
